source: Invested by Aleph — Semiconductors, China/Taiwan fabs, global warming (China's Not Getting Taiwan's Fabs)
summary: Nvidia is extremely difficult to displace as the dominant accelerator platform; attempts to out-GPU the GPU are largely a fool's errand
summary_extended: Baker argues that Nvidia is extraordinarily difficult to displace as the dominant AI accelerator platform, characterizing attempts to replicate GPU capabilities head-on as 'a fool's errand' and stating it is 'very hard to out-Nvidia Nvidia.' His reasoning centers on Nvidia's deep systems and software stack (CUDA ecosystem) which competitors cannot easily replicate — AMD, despite being a good company, lacks this integrated stack. Baker uses historical evidence to support the durability of Nvidia's moat: Google's TPU required three full generations before achieving meaningful success, and AWS Trainium — which he considers the best hyperscaler silicon effort — still needs 2-3 more generations to be competitive. The investment implication is a strong long bias on NVDA, with skepticism toward hyperscaler ASIC programs as near-to-medium-term threats, and limited conviction in AMD as a credible GPU alternative.
detail: Baker argues that hyperscaler internal ASIC programs trying to replicate GPU capabilities head-on will mostly fail. AMD is a good company but lacks Nvidia's systems/software stack. The TPU took three generations to succeed; AWS Trainium is the best hyperscaler silicon effort but still needs 2-3 generations. Jensen Huang is one of the two most exceptional CEOs Baker has encountered.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses strong language ('fool's errand', 'very hard to out-Nvidia Nvidia') and provides historical analogies with high specificity

ticker_audit.reasoning: The entire thesis is about Nvidia's durability as the dominant accelerator platform and the difficulty of displacing it — NVDA is the direct subject.
Baker specifically calls out AMD as a competitor that lacks Nvidia's software/systems stack, making it a direct subject of the competitive displacement thesis.

Baker specifically references Google's TPU program by name, noting it took three generations to succeed — Google is a named example in the thesis, not merely tangential, but as a challenger whose ASIC effort is assessed as slow-moving.
Baker specifically calls out AWS Trainium as the best hyperscaler silicon effort but still needing 2-3 more generations — Amazon is a named subject of the competitive assessment.

Microsoft is not specifically mentioned in this thesis — it appears to have bled in from an adjacent segment. No specific Microsoft ASIC program or competitive claim is made here; remove as tangential.
